title: Permutations and combinations
deck: When choices are made without replacement, the counting principle yields shrinking products. Permutations count ordered arrangements; combinations count unordered selections; the two differ by a factor of r!.
objectives: Distinguish between permutations (order matters) and combinations (order does not), and apply the P(n, r) and C(n, r) formulas to standard selection problems.

content.text: Fifty athletes compete in a single race; gold, silver, and bronze medals are awarded to the first three finishers. In how many distinct ways can the three medals be distributed? The first-place finisher can be any of the 50 athletes; once gold is decided, second place can be any of the remaining 49; once silver is decided, third can be any of the remaining 48. The product is 50 · 49 · 48 = 117,600.

Now consider the seemingly similar question: in how many ways can a three-person subcommittee be chosen from the same 50 athletes, with no roles assigned (all three members are equal)? The committee {Alice, Bob, Carol} is the same committee as {Carol, Bob, Alice}, since the seats are interchangeable. Each committee of three was counted six times by the medal calculation (once for each of the 3! = 6 ways to assign three medals to three specific people), so the committee count is 117,600 / 6 = 19,600.

The same 50 athletes and the same 3 selections produce two very different answers, because the two questions ask for two different counts. The first counts ordered arrangements (permutations); the second counts unordered selections (combinations). This section develops both formulas and the single litmus test that decides which to apply.
6.4.1  Factorials

The factorial of a non-negative integer n, written n!, is the product of all positive integers from 1 up to n. Thus 1! = 1, 2! = 2, 3! = 6, 4! = 24, 5! = 120, and so on; the factorials grow very quickly. By convention, 0! = 1, a definition that simplifies the formulas of this section. The factorial n! counts the number of ways to arrange n distinct objects in a row: the first slot can be any of the n objects, the second any of the remaining n − 1, and so on down to the last slot, which is forced.

Definition 6.3. For any non-negative integer n, the factorial of n is n! = n · (n − 1) · (n − 2) · ··· · 1, with the convention that 0! = 1. The factorial n! is the number of ways to arrange n distinct objects in a row.
6.4.2  Permutations: ordered selections

A permutation of r items chosen from a pool of n distinct items is an ordered selection: the first item picked, the second item picked, and so on. By the fundamental counting principle, the number of permutations is the product of r shrinking factors starting at n: n · (n − 1) · ··· · (n − r + 1). This product has a tidy factorial-quotient form.

Definition 6.4. For non-negative integers n and r with r ≤ n, the number of permutations of r objects chosen from n distinct objects is P(n, r) = n! / (n − r)! = n · (n − 1) · ··· · (n − r + 1). The right-hand product has exactly r factors.

content.caption: Figure 6.4. The same population of 50 athletes yields two different counts. Three distinguishable medals produce P(50, 3) = 117,600 ordered awards; three interchangeable committee seats produce C(50, 3) = 19,600 unordered selections. The combination count is the permutation count divided by r! = 3! = 6.

content.text: 6.4.3  Combinations: unordered selections

A combination of r items chosen from n is an unordered selection: the set of items picked, with no regard for the sequence in which they were picked. Every combination of r items corresponds to r! different permutations of those items (the r! ways to arrange them in a row). To count combinations, we count permutations and then divide by r! to collapse the redundant orderings into single selections.

Definition 6.5. For non-negative integers n and r with r ≤ n, the number of combinations of r objects chosen from n distinct objects is C(n, r) = n! / [(n − r)! · r!] = P(n, r) / r!. The notation C(n, r) is sometimes written &binom;(n, r) or “n choose r.”

Margin noteThe combination count C(n, r) is often called a binomial coefficient. It is the same number that appears as the coefficient of the term xryn−r in the expansion of (x + y)n, a relationship known as the Binomial Theorem. The connection is not a coincidence; both interpretations are counting the same set of selections, just dressed in different algebraic clothing.
The first question to ask on any “how many ways?” problem: does swapping two of my picks give me a different answer? If yes, permutation. If no, combination.

Common mistakeTreating a permutation problem as a combination and vice versa. The phrase “in how many ways” is genuinely ambiguous and decides nothing on its own; the deciding feature is whether the positions or labels in the selection are distinguishable. Medals are distinguishable (gold ≠ silver ≠ bronze) so the medal problem is a permutation; committee seats with no titles are interchangeable so the committee problem is a combination. Always apply the swap test before reaching for a formula.

Example 6.4. Distributing three medals.

Fifty athletes run a race; gold, silver, and bronze medals are awarded to the first three finishers. Assuming no ties, in how many distinct ways can the three medals be distributed?

Solution.

Step 1. Apply the swap test. Swapping the gold and silver medalists changes who receives the gold medal, so it produces a different outcome. Order matters; the problem is a permutation.

Step 2. Identify n and r. Choosing three medalists from 50 athletes gives n = 50 and r = 3.

Step 3. Compute P(n, r).

P(50, 3) = 50 · 49 · 48 = 117,600

There are 117,600 distinct medal distributions for a single 50-athlete race. If the same three athletes had been chosen for a committee with no roles, the count would have been C(50, 3) = 117,600 / 6 = 19,600, smaller by exactly the factor 3!.

In the wildMany lotteries publish their odds in terms of combinations. A typical 6/49 lottery requires the player to choose 6 distinct numbers from 1 to 49; the order of selection is irrelevant. The total number of possible tickets is C(49, 6) = 13,983,816, so the probability of winning the jackpot on a single ticket is roughly 1 in 14 million. The same combinatorial machinery underwrites poker probabilities (a five-card hand is C(52, 5) = 2,598,960), DNA sequence counts in genomics, and pricing of certain financial derivatives.
Exercises

1. Aldo has 15 European cities he would like to visit, but his next vacation has time for only three: one on Monday, one on Tuesday, and one on Wednesday. He will not visit the same city more than once. How many different schedules are possible?

    Show answer

    Answer. 2,730. The days are distinguishable, so order matters: P(15, 3) = 15 · 14 · 13 = 2,730.

  2. A club has 12 members and selects a 3-person subcommittee in which all three roles are equal. How many subcommittees are possible?

    Show answer

    Answer. 220. The seats are interchangeable, so order does not matter: C(12, 3) = (12 · 11 · 10) / 6 = 1,320 / 6 = 220.

  3. A pizzeria offers 10 toppings. How many different 3-topping pizzas can be made, assuming all toppings are added at once and order does not matter?

    Show answer

    Answer. 120. C(10, 3) = (10 · 9 · 8) / 6 = 120.

  4. Compute P(8, 2) and C(8, 2). How are the two numbers related?

    Show answer

    Answer. P(8, 2) = 56 and C(8, 2) = 28. They differ by a factor of 2! = 2, since each combination of 2 items corresponds to 2 different ordered arrangements.

  5. Which of the following is a permutation problem (order matters)?

    1. Choosing 5 books from a shelf to read on vacation
    2. Selecting a 4-card hand from a deck of 52
    3. Assigning 1st, 2nd, and 3rd place to three runners in a race
    4. Picking 3 toppings for a pizza
    Show answer

    Answer. C. The three places are distinguishable, so swapping two assignments produces a different result.

  6. A class of 25 elects a President, Vice President, and Secretary; no student holds more than one office. How many ways can the three offices be filled?

    Show answer

    Answer. 13,800. P(25, 3) = 25 · 24 · 23 = 13,800.
Looking ahead

We now have the tools to count almost any probability sample space we are likely to encounter. Section 6.5 changes register: rather than counting outcomes, we will summarize a random process by a single number, the expected value, that captures the long-run average payout per trial. Expected value is the engine behind insurance pricing, casino edges, and any decision under uncertainty that values long-run outcomes over single-trial ones.
6.4
